items.prompt_md: In the variable-window solution to Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters, when does the **left** edge move?
options: Every time the right edge advances
Only when the incoming character's last-seen index falls inside the current window
Only once, at the very end of the scan
Never — only the right edge moves
items.answer: Only when the incoming character's last-seen index falls inside the current window
items.explanation_md: The left edge is unconditional in the fixed-window lesson, but here it's conditional: it only jumps past a duplicate when the hash map shows that character is still inside `[left, right]`.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning a string once and need the longest contiguous span where no character repeats. The span's start must jump forward whenever a repeat is about to enter. Which approach fits?
options: Grow a window from the right, and shrink its left edge with a hash map whenever the no-repeat constraint breaks
Keep the window a fixed length k and slide it one step at a time
Sort the characters and use two pointers from each end
Try every substring and check each one for duplicates
items.answer: Grow a window from the right, and shrink its left edge with a hash map whenever the no-repeat constraint breaks
items.explanation_md: "Longest span where a condition holds continuously" is the variable-window fingerprint: grow with the right pointer, and use a hash map to detect the exact moment the constraint breaks so the left pointer can shrink past it. Sorting destroys substring order; checking every substring is the brute force this pattern replaces.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the hash-map variable-window solution to Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters?
items.explanation_md: The right pointer visits each character once, and the left pointer only ever moves forward across the whole scan, so total pointer movement is bounded by n — O(n) time and O(min(n, alphabet)) space for the map.
